Title: Thai farmers pin hopes on microbes to end annual burning crisis
Article:
RICE farmers Siriporn and Amnat Taidee used to burn their paddy fields between plantings – a common method of clearing crop residue partly blamed for toxic smog that blankets much of Thailand every spring.
A quick and affordable way to prepare for a new growing cycle, the practice has long been seen as the only feasible option for millions of Thai rice farmers.
But for the couple from Chiang Rai, abandoning what Amnat called "the old way of doing things" for new microbial solutions has been a boon.
Thanks to the hungry bacteria that chew up post-harvest leftovers – their soil is softer, their yields are up and their fertiliser bills are down.
"My life has changed," said Siriporn, 63, as she sloshed through a verdant paddy field in Chiang Rai. "I'm so happy... we don't have to burn anymore."
Every year between January and April, smoke from crop residue, forest fires and industrial emissions – compounded by smog drifting over from neighbouring countries – pushes Thailand's air quality to dangerous levels.
Bangkok has restricted burning for years, but a recent crackdown has put government environmental goals on a collision course with traditional agricultural practices.
The threat of heavy fines and even prison has frightened farmers – but many feel they still have no alternative.
"It simply pushes the burden onto farmers," Witsanu Attavanich, an environmental economist at Kasetsart University, said of the ban on open burning.
The Taidees were early converts, using a product called Soil Digest developed by a Thai scientist using five strains of Bacillus bacteria – one of which is derived from traditional fermented soybeans.
Siriporn said the solution decomposed the "terrible" stubble on the fields in a matter of days and helped restore the soil.
"The rice is coming in great and the soil is healthy," she says. "This microbial stuff is a game-changer."
[[nid:1419257]]
Last year, authorities in Chiang Rai – one of Thailand's main rice-growing provinces – began encouraging local farmers to try microbial solutions.
So far, around 2,000 have made the switch – a fraction of the province's 100,000 rice farmers, but a start.
"If we are to stop the burning, we must provide them with multiple alternatives," said Orracha Wongsaroj, a provincial agricultural official.
Microbial products have long been used in Thai agriculture, but demand for newer formulations targeting straw decomposition has grown sharply since the burning crackdown intensified a few years ago.
The Thai government promotes free access to microbial products for farmers – but officials told AFP that stocks had run out and they were struggling to scale up supply.
For those who cannot access government supplies, private market alternatives can be pricey.
In Pathum Thani, a rice-growing province in central Thailand, farmer Samart Atthong spent 1,200 baht (US$37) hiring an agricultural drone to spray Soil Digest over his fields.
"People may only see the extra cost of the microbes, but they should look in the long run," Samart said.
"Once the soil heals, we won't need nearly as much fertiliser," he said. "Where I live, burning has dropped to nearly zero."
Striding through air thick with dust from yeast, retired professor Wichien Yongmanitchai watches the whirring machines producing his microbial concoction.
He started by isolating local bacterial strains, convinced that native ones would work best in Thailand's tropical environment.
[[nid:1419256]]
He hopes his invention – sold as Soil Digest – will fix Thailand's annual air crisis for good.
Without microbial treatment, rice straw takes around 30 days to soften enough to till – his solution works in five to seven days. Early trials show yield increases of up to 20 per cent.
Wichien also said the bacteria can reduce methane emissions from paddy fields by at least 20 per cent, helping Thailand meet its climate commitments.
"This is one of the biggest benefits to make (for achieving) carbon neutrality in the rice field."
But Wichien knows his small operation cannot reach Thailand's 20 million farmers alone. And without government and corporate backing, the gap may prove too wide.
Independent experts agree the technology shows promise – but warn the system around it must change.
"The government wants to reduce straw burning – but there is no one-size-fits-all solution," said Nipon Poapongsakorn, an agricultural policy expert at the Thailand Development Research Institute.
To start with he suggests conditional subsidies tied to a no-burn commitment, combined with machinery access and farmer education.
Wichien is already thinking beyond Thailand to paddy fields across Southeast Asia and ultimately Africa.
"I don't want anything else. I just want to make it work."
* The writer is from AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Police cripple violent Sarawak gang, 21 arrested
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Police have crippled a violent gang operating in Sarawak with the arrest of 21 local men in a coordinated crackdown under Op Pusak Merah.
The operation, launched on April 15, was carried out jointly by Bukit Aman's Criminal Investigation Department (CID) D9 unit and the Sarawak CID, targeting suspects linked to a string of violent crimes since 2024.
CID director Datuk M. Kumar said the suspects, aged between 19 and 44, were detained in separate raids across Kota Samarahan, Kuching, Simunjan and Padawan.
MORE TO COME
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Johor Immigration denies closing counters for Raya potluck
Article:
JOHOR BARU: The Johor Immigration Department has denied viral social media claims that its counters at Setia Tropika here were closed for an internal Hari Raya potluck party.
Its director Datuk Mohd Rusdi Mohd Darus said the counters operated as usual and no closures were implemented for the festivities.
"There were no instructions to close the counters; it was only an internal potluck Raya celebration.
"There may have been Raya music playing. It did not involve outside guests and was conducted in a controlled manner," he said today.
He added that any counter closures would typically be accompanied by an official notification.
Earlier, a woman shared a photo on Threads showing empty counters at the department, alleging that staff were holding a party during working hours.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Uzma order book to hit up to RM4.5bil on new contracts
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Uzma Bhd's order book is estimated to rise to between RM4.3 billion and RM4.5 billion after securing three coiled tubing unit (CTU) contracts from Petronas Carigali Sdn Bhd, providing earnings visibility of up to five years.
Public Investment Bank Bhd (PublicInvest) said that while the contract value was not disclosed due to its call-out nature, it estimates a potential value of RM500 million to RM700 million over the contract period.
The estimate is benchmarked against similar CTU contracts secured in November 2020 and December 2022.
"The contract reinforces Uzma's position as a key well services provider to Petronas Carigali, supported by its established asset base, operational readiness and ability to execute complex intervention programmes, including in high-pressure, high-temperature environments.
"We view this as a continuation of Uzma's strong track record in upstream services, particularly in production optimisation and brownfield activities, which remain critical amid sustained upstream capital expenditure and elevated oil prices," it said in a note.
PublicInvest said the contract win comes at a favourable time, with the monsoon season ending and offshore activities entering a seasonal peak, supported by higher oil prices amid ongoing tensions in the Middle East.
The firm maintained its "Outperform" call on Uzma, with an unchanged target price of 56 sen, based on 5.3 times its financial year 2027 earnings per share.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Muhyiddin trial: Bankers detail RM5mil transactions to Bersatu
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Bersatu received RM5 million from several entities in 2021 and 2022, the Special Corruption High Court heard today.
The evidence on the payments was confirmed by two bankers, CIMB Bukit Tunku branch manager Herdayanti Md Hassan, and Maybank Kota Kemuning assistant branch manager Alfian Othman.
The duo was testifying in the case against former prime minister Tan Sri Muhyiddin Yassin, who faces seven charges involving four counts of abuse of power and three counts of money laundering linked to the Jana Wibawa project.
Herdayanti, 44, said four transactions involving RM2 million, RM1 million, and two payments of RM500,000 each were made via cheques issued in the name of businessman Datuk Azman Yusoff between Sept 24, 2021, and Nov 3, 2022.
The seventh prosecution witness said, based on CIMB Bank statements belonging to Azman registered under KCJ Engineering Sdn Bhd, the funds were withdrawn from the businessman's account.
During cross-examination, Herdayanti, who was referred to several transaction documents cross-checked with cheques issued to Bersatu, was asked by defence lawyer Datuk Amer Hamzah Arshad whether she knew the purpose of the cheques.
However, she replied that she was unsure.
Meanwhile, Alfian, 57, said in his testimony that on Feb 21, 2022, Nepturis Sdn Bhd made a RM1 million transaction via cheque, and based on the cheque image documents, it was paid to Bersatu.
Under examination-in-chief by deputy public prosecutor Wan Nur Iman Wan Ahmad Afzal, the eighth prosecution witness said he had been instructed by the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) to prepare documents for the trial.
During cross-examination, Alfian agreed with Amer Hamzah's suggestion that the transaction was not made for Muhyiddin personally.
Amer Hamzah: If we refer to all bank statements provided by Maybank to the MACC, I suggest that not a single sen was paid from Nepturis to my client.
Alfian: Agreed.
Amer Hamzah: And it was only payments to Bersatu?
Alfian: Agreed.
Amer Hamzah: The cheque payments involving Nepturis were recorded, transparent, and nothing was concealed.
Alfian: Agreed.
Muhyiddin, 79, has become the second Malaysian prime minister to sit in the dock facing corruption charges, after Datuk Seri Najib Razak, whose cases linked to the 1Malaysia Development Bhd scandal had dominated the courts for years.
He allegedly abused his position to solicit RM232.5 million for Bersatu and faces three money laundering charges involving RM200 million deposited into two bank accounts.
The trial before Judge Noor Ruwena Md Nurdin continues on April 28.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: UiTM vice-chancellor receives national education leadership award
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Universiti Teknologi Mara (UiTM) vice-chancellor, Prof Datuk Dr Shahrin Sahib, has received the Malaysian Outstanding Education Icon Leadership Excellence Award.
The award was presented to him at the 9th Malaysian Education & Learning Summit 2026 at the Majestic Hotel Kuala Lumpur today.
The ceremony was officiated by Education Ministry deputy director-general (policy and curriculum sector) Dr Rusmini Ku Ahmad, who represented Education Minister Fadhlina Sidek.
The award recognised Shahrin's role in strengthening institutional direction, advancing academic excellence and supporting broader education development through inclusive and innovation-driven leadership.
Shahrin said leadership in higher education goes beyond administrative responsibilities.
"Leadership in higher education is about creating impact beyond the university, including nurturing individuals who were resilient, ethical and ready to contribute to society," he said.
Under his leadership, UiTM continued to strengthen its position as one of Malaysia's largest public universities, focusing on governance, talent development and inclusive access to higher education.
UiTM said the recognition reflects its continued efforts to uphold excellence in leadership, governance and institutional impact, while reaffirming its commitment to advancing innovation and transformation in the higher education sector.
The summit was organised by the KSI Strategic Institute for Asia Pacific (KSI) in collaboration with the Malaysian Association of Private Colleges and Universities and the National Association of Private Educational Institutions.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Perdana Petroleum secures Petronas Carigali work orders
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Perdana Petroleum Bhd has secured two work orders from Petronas Carigali Sdn Bhd for the provision of anchor handling tug and supply vessels.
In a filing with Bursa Malaysia, the group said the contracts were awarded to its wholly-owned subsidiary, Perdana Nautika Sdn Bhd.
The vessels will be chartered for 303 days and 224 days respectively, with an option to extend each charter by up to 40 days.
The contract value has yet to be determined, as it will depend on work orders issued by the charterer during the contract period.
Perdana Nautika will provide the vessels, along with crew and equipment, to support round-the-clock operations, including drilling rigs, offshore installations, derrick barges, as well as towing and anchor handling services.
The contracts are expected to contribute positively to the group's earnings and net assets for the financial years ending Dec 31, 2026 and 2027, excluding any extension period.
Perdana shares closed unchanged at 16 sen, giving it a market capitalisation of RM356.42 million.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia on track for AI nation status by 2030 despite Middle East conflict
Article:
ISKANDAR PUTERI: The Digital Ministry remains confident that Malaysia is on track to achieve its vision of becoming an artificial intelligence (AI) nation by 2030, despite the ongoing conflict in the Middle East.
Digital Minister Gobind Singh Deo said the country must nonetheless prepare for any future impacts that could affect the vision.
"We are still on track. For now, we are making the necessary preparations to ensure our vision of becoming an AI nation by 2030 is achieved.
"We will make the required preparations should there be any effects that might affect this vision in the future, but for now, we remain on track," he told reporters after launching the Malaysia Digital Economy Corporation (MDEC) Southern Region Digital Economy office here today.
Also present was MDEC chief executive officer Anuar Fariz Fadzil.
Malaysia is currently implementing the national AI roadmap to achieve its 2030 aspirations, focusing on technology development, talent, governance, infrastructure, and sustainable investment.
The goal involves building a comprehensive ecosystem that combines policy, digital trust, and strategic investment to position Malaysia as a regional AI hub.
Gobind said the target for the digital economy to contribute 30 per cent to the Gross Domestic Product (GDP) by 2030 remains unchanged.
He noted that the interim target of a 25.5 per cent contribution to the GDP by 2025 is also on track.
"One of our visions is the 30 per cent digital economy contribution to the GDP, which remains. For 2025, our target of 25.5 per cent is also on track.
"So there is no problem, we just need to move forward towards the 30 per cent target by 2030," he said.
Commenting on the impact of the Middle East conflict on the digital sector, including data centre development, Gobind said projects are proceeding as planned.
He said many agreements have been signed and work has commenced, with some projects already completed.
Some projects have started operations, while others are under construction or in the midst of agreement finalisation. Therefore, it continues as usual, he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Crisis offers chance for businesses to reposition, says Steven Sim
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A challenging global environment is not all downside for local businesses, with entrepreneurs urged to view current pressures as an opportunity to reposition and grow.
Entrepreneur Development and Cooperatives Minister Steven Sim Chee Keong said that despite concerns over subsidy limits and rising costs, substantial government support remains in place to help businesses navigate the environment.
"I am not saying that everything is smooth. Indeed, the world, not only Malaysia, is facing significant challenges," he said after Bank Kerjasama Rakyat Malaysia Bhd's financial performance and dividend announcement today.
"At the same time, excessive pessimism does not help. The government continues to commit significant resources to subsidies and cash assistance."
Sim said programmes such as Sumbangan Tunai Rahmah and Sumbangan Asas Rahmah amount to nearly RM15 billion under the 2026 Budget, while wage growth in both the public and private sectors is supporting domestic consumption.
On fuel, he said the scale of subsidies is often underestimated.
"All private vehicles owned by Malaysians are still enjoying fuel at RM1.99 per litre. It is a huge buffer to protect ordinary Malaysians," he said.
He added that up to 80 per cent of diesel usage remains subsidised, benefiting more than 400,000 commercial vehicles, including lorries, taxis and food trucks, which continue to pay around RM2.15 per litre despite higher market prices.
However, Sim stressed that long-term resilience cannot rely solely on subsidies.
"When pricing is no longer our competitive advantage, we must focus on productivity and move up the value chain," he said.
To support this shift, the ministry is expanding its PowerUp 10,000 initiative, with at least RM15 billion in financing to be channelled to small and medium enterprises (SMEs) this year, up from RM10 billion previously.
"This funding is there to support working capital and keep businesses running.
"In a crisis, there are opportunities. We want this financing to also support companies to expand and grow, and to take advantage of 'US plus one', 'China plus one', and even 'Dubai plus one' strategies," he added.
Sim said that while global costs such as energy and commodities remain beyond domestic control, efforts are under way to ease cost pressures at the operational level.
He said engagements with financial institutions have led to greater flexibility for affected businesses.
"Companies can approach banks on a case-by-case basis for restructuring, refinancing, or even a moratorium," he said, adding that discussions are ongoing with Tenaga Nasional Bhd to explore ways to manage energy-related costs for SMEs.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: DBKL, CIMB partner to boost KL's community, economic growth
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Dewan Bandaraya Kuala Lumpur (DBKL) and CIMB Group have announced a collaboration aimed at strengthening economic growth and community development in the capital.
Dubbed "Moving KL Forward – Kita Bagi Jadi", the initiative was formalised through a three-year memorandum of collaboration signed today.
CIMB Islamic Bhd chief executive officer Ahmad Shahriman Mohd Shariff said CIMB has allocated RM5 million over three years for the initiative, which will be implemented in collaboration with DBKL through carefully planned programmes.
"The allocation will not be limited to a single initiative. CIMB remains open to expanding cooperation into other areas such as park development and community projects, building on existing partnerships with the local authority.
"CIMB Islamic has previously collaborated with DBKL on initiatives such as Taman Tugu and the adoption of public housing projects under its community programmes," he said at a press conference here.
Witnessing the signing ceremony were Minister in the Prime Minister's Department (Federal Territories) Hannah Yeoh, Kuala Lumpur mayor Datuk Fadlun Mak Ujud and CIMB Group chairman Datuk Syed Zaid Albar.
Yeoh said there is no rigid KPI framework for the initiative, as the focus is on public engagement and community enjoyment of the programmes.
"As long as people enjoy themselves, participate in the programmes and benefit from them, I believe the objectives are achieved," she said, adding that feedback and participation levels will guide future improvements.
She said the initiative aims to create a vibrant city experience, encourage more community activities and ensure Kuala Lumpur remains inclusive for all groups.
"It is better than doing nothing. We are in an exploratory phase and, as long as the public benefits and enjoys the activities, then we are achieving our purpose of making KL lively," she added.
The "Moving KL Forward – Kita Bagi Jadi" initiative also aims to elevate arts, culture and heritage while creating economic opportunities for communities, youth and entrepreneurs.
Syed Zaid said it will be highlighted through monthly festivals and concerts beginning in May, including the "Moving KL Forward Concert at the Park" at Panggung Anniversary featuring the DBKL Orchestra and local performers.
He added that the programme will culminate in a Merdeka Eve Concert at Dataran Merdeka in August, which is expected to attract about 80,000 visitors.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Felda eyes wider use of B100 biodiesel
Article:
KUANTAN: The rollout of B100, a 100 per cent palm oil-based biodiesel, is set to expand beyond Federal Land Development Authority (Felda) vehicles to include plantation machinery.
Felda chairman Datuk Seri Ahmad Shabery Cheek said discussions on extending the use of B100 to palm oil processing machinery at Felda schemes were held last week, with Kuantan likely to be the starting point as early as this month.
"Processed at its commercial arm FGV Holdings Berhad's factory in Kuantan, logistics will be more efficient there before distributing B100 nationwide. However, regulatory requirements, including storage facilities and licensing at other Felda schemes, still need to be addressed.
"B100 is not yet commercially available as it remains at the policy stage, but efforts are ongoing to build public confidence and position it as a viable and sustainable alternative," he told reporters after attending a briefing on B100.
He said there was a time when many paid little attention to B100, which was priced at RM4.40 per litre, compared with about RM3.50 for regular diesel.
"People were previously reluctant to talk about biodiesel because it was perceived as expensive. Despite being environmentally friendly and a cleaner option, low awareness and cost concerns limited interest.
"Now, the situation is changing as diesel prices have risen to nearly RM7.00, making alternatives like B100 more affordable," he said.
FGV is one of the world's largest palm oil producers. Palm oil is used to make cooking oil, processed foods, cosmetics, detergents and biofuels.
Ahmad Shabery said expanded use of B100 would benefit farmers, smallholders and Felda settlers, as higher usage could help boost palm oil prices and improve livelihoods.
"B100 will benefit the fishing community, especially Zone C2 fishermen, as well as transport operators," he said.
On commercialisation, he said it would take time, as increasing production capacity requires more processing plants to convert crude palm oil (CPO) into biodiesel.
"The existing facilities can only accommodate around 100,000 to 180,000 tonnes, and we are still in the early stages.
"Expanding this capacity will take time, as designing and building new plants requires significant investment and development," he said.
On April 13, Malaysian Palm Oil Board (MPOB) director-general Datuk Ahmad Parveez Ghulam Kadir said B100 offers a potentially cheaper alternative to conventional diesel and helps reduce dependence on imported diesel.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Family wants inspector in attempted murder case of cop placed in hospital
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The family of a police inspector charged with attempted murder has taken the position that he should not be granted bail, but should instead be placed in a hospital for treatment.
Their wish was conveyed by defence lawyer Nanda Goban after Muhammad Kamal Raj Shanmugam Abdullah, 40, was charged before Sessions Court judge Mazuliana Abdul Rashid.
Mazuliana ordered Kamal to be sent to Hospital Bahagia Ulu Kinta in Perak for a one-month psychiatric evaluation.
The lawyer said he had received instructions from his client's family not to apply for bail.
"It is our and the family's position that he be placed in a hospital. It is safer than being at home," he said after proceedings.
He added that Kamal has been suffering from schizophrenia for the past three years and has been on medical leave for at least two years, with his last medical certificate valid until February.
Kamal, he said, is unmarried and was previously based at the Kedah police headquarters.
Asked why he was in Kuala Lumpur, Nanda said his client was on medical leave and has family residing in Cheras.
Nanda also revealed that Kamal's father had, on two occasions, sought police assistance to bring his son to the station.
"He was not taking his medication and his condition was worsening.
"The father lodged a police report and asked the police to take him to the hospital.
"The police could not arrest him as he had not committed any offence.
"They tried to persuade him, but in the process he ran away. This happened recently.
"I do not blame the police as they could not arrest him but were merely assisting the father," he said.
Earlier, the magistrate's court, where Kamal was set to be charged with seven counts of reckless and dangerous driving, adopted the Sessions Court's decision to have him sent to the hospital.
Magistrate Tengku Syazwany Yasmyn Tuan Roslan made the decision after she was informed by both the prosecution and defence regarding Kamal's mental state.
The charge was read out in court; however, Nanda addressed the court before Kamal entered his plea.
"The position of the defence is that he is unfit to plead by virtue of his schizophrenia condition," the lawyer said.
Kamal was charged with driving in a reckless and dangerous manner that put a man at risk about 4.30pm along Jalan Loke Yew on April 13 under Section 42(1) of the Road Transport Act.
A conviction under this section carries a jail term of up to five years and a fine of between RM5,000 and RM15,000 for a first offence, while repeat offences may result in imprisonment of up to 10 years and a fine of between RM10,000 and RM20,000.
He further noted that the Sessions Court, after hearing submissions, ordered that Kamal be sent to the hospital for psychiatric assessment.
Deputy public prosecutor Nurbatrisyia Mohd Razib, referring to the investigation papers, said a medical report indicated that the accused has a mental condition. She requested that he be sent for a psychiatric assessment to determine his mental state.
The magistrate then adopted the Sessions Court order for all charges and set June 19 for mention.
Both courts did not record any plea.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 299 fake energy crisis posts removed, Facebook and TikTok top list
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: Social media platforms have removed 299 pieces of fake content related to the global energy crisis following requests from the Malaysian Communications and Multimedia Commission (MCMC).
Communications Minister Datuk Fahmi Fadzil said the figure represents a 66 per cent "take down" rate out of 450 identified items flagged between March 29 and 7am today.
"The rest are still in the process of being removed because taking down content sometimes takes some time at the platform level," he told reporters after the Communications Ministry's monthly assembly today.
Fahmi noted that 147 pieces of content specifically concerned the Strait of Hormuz, with 122 of those removed by this morning.
He identified Facebook as the primary source of misinformation with 180 flagged items, followed by TikTok with 134.
The minister added that an increase in false content on Threads was likely due to the interconnected nature of Facebook and Instagram accounts, which simplified the account creation process for users.
Authorities have opened 57 investigation papers regarding the dissemination of false information linked to the energy crisis.
"One case is set for prosecution in the near future, while five cases have received compounding orders," Fahmi said, adding that the compound amounts have yet to be determined.
Also present were Deputy Communications Minister Datuk Teo Nie Ching, Communications Ministry secretary-general Datuk Abdul Halim Hamzah and Malaysian National News Agency (Bernama) chief executive officer Datin Paduka Nur-ul Afida Kamaludin.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: In tough times, Russians are turning to magic
Article:
SELF-DESCRIBED witch Natalia Malinovskaya has a new client base: men fighting in eastern Ukraine, who, like a growing number of Russians, are drawn to the supernatural against a backdrop of conflict and economic uncertainty.
In her darkened apartment in Moscow, Malinovskaya, who says she inherited her powers from her grandmother, offers services from love spells to protection from evil.
She has also made frequent appearances on Russian television.
Demonstrating how she detects a bad vibe over someone's health by wafting a lit match over a glass, she says most people seek help for love problems, including soldiers worried about their partners staying faithful.
"They contact me, and there are many of them," said Malinovskaya, cautioning that she can only cast spells in person when soldiers are on leave.
"It's impossible to carry out a ritual at the front. Where would someone burn candles, and how would I even send them there?"
Orthodox Christianity, mysticism and folk superstitions have coexisted in Russia through centuries of tsarist rule and the religious repression of the Soviet era.
Interest surged in the final years of the Russian Empire - when faith healer Rasputin's influence over the tsar's family caused public outrage - and again during the chaotic years after the Soviet Union's collapse.
After a period of decline it is on the rise again.
"Today's geopolitical and economic challenges in Russia and worldwide heighten anxiety, triggering a surge in mysticism," state pollster VTsIOM said in March as it published a poll showing that 85 per cent of Russians had dabbled in magical practices.
"In such conditions, especially against the backdrop of military threats, belief (regardless of which gods are involved) turns into a tool of psychological defense," it said.
[[nid:1419210]]
Russian forces have been on the offensive in Ukraine for over four years, fuelling a deadly crisis that has damaged Russia's international standing, slowed the economy and driven up the cost of living.
Nearly half of Russians believe that some people may be able to predict the future or have magical powers, the survey showed, up from less than a third in 2019.
Demand for crystal balls and protective amulets more than doubled last year, while sales of aspen stakes - said to defend the owner from evil spirits - quadrupled, said Russian cash register operator ATOL, citing consumer spending data.
At Witch Store in Moscow, which sells crystals, tarot guides, and other magical paraphernalia, a couple of customers browsed the shelves looking for incense to cleanse the air or bring good luck.
"Black obsidian spheres are very popular and in high demand. Obsidian is considered a stone of safety," said the shop's co-owner, Yulia Gusanova, referring to volcanic glass.
Voodoo-themed bar Marie Laveau, which offers tarot readings, has benefited from the zeitgeist, said owner Evgeniya Chassagnard.
"It turned out that we matched perfectly with the moment," she said.
The revival has its opponents. Last year, a group of lawmakers submitted a bill to ban adverts for services such as astrology and energy healing, warning that they can lead to the financial exploitation of vulnerable people.
Patriarch Kirill, the head of Russia's Orthodox Church, backed the idea of a ban last year and in January decried the "mass manipulative influence" of fortune-tellers and psychics.
"There is a dark force present in fortune-telling. If miracles involve divine power and grace, then fortune-telling involves a devilish power," he told state news agency TASS in a separate interview, implying a link with "satanism" which was outlawed by the Supreme Court last year.
Asked if the church, which sometimes performs exorcisms, considered psychics as rivals, a spokesman for the church, Vakhtang Kupshidze, told Reuters that this was missing the point.
"We don't exactly see them as competitors, because competition implies ... a struggle for clients," he said.
* The article is from Reuters
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Experts back move to align diesel subsidy with Budi95 system
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Experts have lauded the government's move to review the mechanism for targeted diesel subsidies, aligning it with the system currently implemented under the Budi95 initiative for RON95 petrol.
The Budi95 initiative utilises MyKad for targeted fuel distribution nationwide, with a monthly purchase quota of 200 litres per person, aimed at reducing leakages and ensuring subsidies reach intended recipients.
University Utara Malaysia (UUM) economist Dr Irwan Shah Zainal Abidin said extending the Budi95 mechanism to diesel was both necessary and practical, given its effectiveness in curbing subsidy leakages.
"For diesel, prices have already been floated, except in Sabah, Sarawak and Labuan. So, I think the time has come for the government to consider this, especially as we expect the conflict in the Middle East to persist," he told the New Straits Times.
Irwan said the Budi95 system has helped reduce leakages, particularly when there is a wide gap between market prices and subsidised rates.
However, he cautioned against oversimplifying the situation or underestimating the uncertainties surrounding the current global environment.
"There are still many variables that remain unclear. However, it is better to act early rather than wait until conditions worsen.
"At this stage, the government still has room to act, and one way is by expanding the Budi mechanism to diesel," he added.
Irwan also said recent measures to adjust Budi95 quota limits from 300 litres to 200 litres were appropriate with further refinements may be needed depending on developments.
Meanwhile, UUM security studies expert Dr Faisol Keling said implementing a MyKad-based diesel subsidy mechanism could help stabilise prices and curb illicit activities.
"It would be good for the government to extend Budi Diesel to the rakyat using the Mykad mechanism. This can help stabilise prices of goods and the cost of living, which are currently affected by high diesel prices.
"Key sectors such as agriculture and services have been impacted by rising costs. While Budi95 helps stabilise daily consumption, diesel subsidies are crucial to stabilise broader economic activities," he said.
Faisol added that a more targeted system could also reduce incentives for cross-border fuel smuggling, particularly in high-risk areas.
"This particular implementation should also be applied nationwide," he said.
Yesterday, Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim has instructed the Finance Ministry to review the mechanism for targeted diesel subsidies, in line with systems currently implemented under the Budi95 initiative.
Communications Minister Datuk Fahmi Fadzil said the findings of the review were expected to be presented next week, either at a
National Economic Action Council (NEAC) meeting
or at a cabinet meeting.
The evaluation of the diesel subsidy mechanism comes amid global energy instability, exacerbated by geopolitical friction in the Middle East involving Iran, the United States, and Israel.
The subsequent disruption of maritime traffic through the Strait of Hormuz has significantly impacted the international trade of refined petroleum products.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Unlicensed welfare home operator faces fresh child abuse charges
Article:
GOMBAK: A 31-year-old man, who was previously charged with sexual assault, was brought before the Selayang Sessions and Magistrate's Courts today to face five new charges of abusing residents and operating an unlicensed children's welfare home.
S. Valan, 31, the operator of the home, pleaded not guilty to all charges read before Sessions Court Judge Norazlin Othman and magistrate Chai Guan Hock.
In the Sessions Court, Valan was charged with abusing four children under his care—two boys aged 11 and 17, and two girls aged 8 and 16—resulting in physical injuries.
The offences were allegedly committed at the premises in Batu Caves between 2023 and 2025.
The charges were framed under Section 31(1)(a) of the Child Act 2001, which carries a maximum fine of RM50,000, up to 20 years' imprisonment, or both, upon conviction.
Deputy public prosecutor Kamarul Iman Ahmad Sapian proposed a high bail amount, citing the seriousness of the case and the significant public interest following its viral spread on social media.
Lawyer Nur Amalia Daud, representing Valan, pleaded for a minimum amount, stating her client supports his parents, wife, and three children, and is willing to comply with additional court conditions.
The court allowed bail at RM40,000 with one surety and ordered the accused to report to a police station monthly and refrain from contacting witnesses.
Norazlin fixed June 18 for mention.
Meanwhile, in the magistrate's court, Valan pleaded not guilty to operating an unregistered childcare centre at the same location about 8pm on April 3.
The charge, under Section 5(1) of the Care Centres Act 1993, carries a maximum fine of RM10,000, up to two years' jail, or both. A daily fine of up to RM1,000 may also be imposed for each day the offence continues.
Chai allowed bail at RM2,500 with one surety and set June 19 for mention.
On April 9,
the accused pleaded not guilty in the Ampang Sessions Court to four counts of physical sexual assault against four boys, aged 13 to 16, at the same home and a premises in Kalumpang between 2019 and last month.
Those charges were framed under Sections 14(a) and 14(b) of the Sexual Offences Against Children Act 2017, which carry a maximum of 20 years' imprisonment and whipping.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Azalina: Parliament role risks political influence in Public Prosecutor appointment
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Politics will inevitably influence the appointment of a Public Prosecutor should Parliament play a role in the selection process following the separation of powers from the Attorney-General's Chambers.
Minister in the Prime Minister's Department (Law and Institutional Reform) Datuk Seri Azalina Othman Said said this was because Parliament comprised politicians.
She said the government was weighing whether the proposed constitutional amendment would require such political involvement.
Azalina said the government aimed to find a middle ground by assessing demands from non-governmental organisations (NGOs) and civil society organisations (CSOs), which want Parliament to play a larger role in the appointment process.
"That (demand) is reasonable and fair, as they view Parliament as the voice of the people.
"However, we must also look at comparisons with other countries. In some nations, even though Parliament is involved, the executive still proposes the candidates," she said.
She said various options were being considered as the government researched how to strike a balance.
"Like it or not, when we involve Parliament, we cannot escape the influence of political parties because Parliament consists of members of political parties.
"The question is whether we want politicians involved or not," she told reporters here.
Earlier, Azalina chaired the third Special Select Committee meeting of 2026 on the Constitutional Amendment regarding the Separation of the Roles of the Attorney-General and the Public Prosecutor.
Committee members and other members of parliament from both sides of the divide were present.
On March 3, the Dewan Rakyat agreed to refer the bill to the committee, which comprises 11 MPs from the government and opposition blocs, for detailed scrutiny.
Meanwhile, Azalina said all political party representatives in the committee had been directed to submit their official stances on the proposed amendment.
"We need to know their official views on this appointment issue before further decisions are made," she said.
She added that inputs from NGOs and CSOs covered the appointment and termination of service, and the need for the body to be accountable to Parliament.
Azalina said the fourth meeting on April 27 would examine views from the Attorney-General's Chambers regarding legal aspects before a final draft was completed.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Govt to enforce under-16 social media ban by June
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: The government plans to restrict social media access for children under 16 by late June as part of a major push to strengthen online protection.
Communications Minister Datuk Fahmi Fadzil said the move is currently subject to a final review, with the Malaysian Communications and Multimedia Commission (MCMC) conducting a regulatory sandbox with social media platforms.
The sandbox aims to identify current industry mechanisms and standardise them across all service providers.
"We are not looking at just 'age assurance', but 'age verification', as Malaysia's legal framework differs in that we have official government documents such as the MyKad," he said after the ministry's monthly assembly today.
Fahmi noted that MCMC held in-depth discussions with platform representatives in Singapore last Friday.
"They are currently reviewing the matter and will present it to me, possibly within the next week. We hope to implement the restrictions around June or July," he said.
He added that several platforms have expressed readiness to implement the minimum age limit for new account registrations.
The move follows a global trend of tightening social media regulations for minors, most notably in Australia, which moved to ban users under 16 last year, citing negative impacts on young users.
Also present were Deputy Communications Minister Datuk Teo Nie Ching, Communications Ministry secretary-general Datuk Abdul Halim Hamzah and Bernama chief executive officer Datin Paduka Nur-ul Afida Kamaludin.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: South Korea, China to hold high-level economic talks next week amid Middle East crisis
Article:
SEOUL: South Korea and China will hold senior-level economic talks early next week to discuss joint efforts to ensure a stable environment for trade and investment amid the Middle East crisis, the foreign ministry said Thursday, Yonhap News Agency reported.
Second Vice Foreign Minister Kim Jina will join Chinese Vice Commerce Minister Yan Dong in Beijing on Monday for the 29th South Korea-China Joint Economic Commission, Park Il, the ministry spokesperson, said in a press briefing.
"In particular, the vice ministers are expected to discuss ways to work together to overcome the crisis, including expanding bilateral trade and investment and fostering a stable and predictable business environment, amid a global polycrisis triggered by the Middle East conflict," Park said.
Next week's meeting will also address efforts to stably manage supply chains and deepen multilateral cooperation in the region, he added.
Established in 1992, the joint commission has served as a bilateral dialogue channel between Seoul and Beijing for broad talks on economy-related issues of mutual concern and efforts to promote economic cooperation.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Selangor unveils RM130.4mil economic package to cushion Middle East crisis impact
Article:
SHAH ALAM: The Selangor government today unveiled the first phase of its economic package involving an RM130.42 million allocation to mitigate the economic impact of the ongoing Middle East crisis.
Menteri Besar Datuk Seri Amirudin Shari said the package, dubbed Pengukuhan Daya Tahan Selangor, is aimed at strengthening food security, easing the cost of living and preventing excessive inflation.
He said the initiatives were also designed to increase the use of public transport and provide targeted assistance to the most affected groups in the state.
"The allocation for the Jelajah Ehsan Rahmah programme through the Selangor Agricultural Development Corporation has been increased by RM5 million, bringing the total to RM10 million this year.
"This is expected to benefit 168,000 low-income households," he said at a press conference.
To ensure sufficient food supplies, the state government will allocate RM50 million for the Gudang Makanan Selangor (GMS) initiative, with RM25 million from state funds and another RM25 million from the corporation's reserves.
"This ensures that if food imports are disrupted, Selangor can activate the GMS to provide essential stocks such as rice and protein, while preventing raw material prices from soaring," he said.
The state government has also allocated RM30 million for the Kita Selangor voucher programme, which provides RM100 monthly aid for six months to 50,000 people.
"This is to ensure that no one falls into absolute poverty should they lose their jobs or source of income this year due to inflationary pressures," he said.
In a move to ease parents' expenses, the state's 12 local authorities will provide free breakfast assistance to all 873 schools in Selangor.
Beginning in August, one school in each local authority area will receive aid daily, involving a total allocation of RM9.6 million.
To encourage the use of public transport and reduce commuting costs, 50,000 residents will receive RM30 a month for six months to be used for Demand-Responsive Transit (DRT), buses or trains. This involves an allocation of RM5.4 million, bringing the total public transport support to RM10.4 million.
For the primary sector, RM25 million has been set aside to cover agricultural input costs or diesel prices. This will benefit 17,237 padi farmers, fishermen, livestock breeders and aquaculture operators, with estimated assistance ranging from RM300 to RM600 a month for six months.
In addition, RM6 million has been allocated to empower entrepreneurs in the retail and logistics sectors, including development grants for cooperatives through Koperasi Warga Hijrah (Kohijrah).
Amirudin said the allocations were decided during the state executive council meeting.
"Even if the conflict subsides, the recovery of the crude oil, gas, fertiliser, sulphur and helium ecosystems — all of which are critical to Selangor's industries — is expected to take at least a year to return to pre-war production levels," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] Armed gang flees with RM9.7mil in jewellery after Brickfields heist
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Four armed men made off with jewellery worth RM9.7 million after robbing a jewellery shop in Brickfields today.
City police chief Datuk Fadil Marsus said the incident occurred around 11am today at a premises along Jalan Tun Sambanthan, when four unidentified suspects entered the store armed with two pistols.
"The suspects are believed to have taken 21 trays of gold jewellery, weighing an estimated 15kg, before fleeing the scene.
"A shotgun and a round of ammunition belonging to a security guard were also taken during the incident," he said in a statement.
Fadil said investigations are ongoing and efforts are underway to track down all suspects involved.
He added that the case is being investigated under Sections 395 and 397 of the Penal Code for gang and armed robbery which carries a punishment of up to 20 years in prison and caning (whipping) upon conviction.
Those with information can contact the Brickfields police hotline at 03-2297 9222, the City police hotline at 03-2115 9999, or any nearby police station.
Earlier, video of the incident went viral where CCTV footage shows the incident unfolding about 10.48am, when four masked men arrived at the location in a white Nissan Almera.
The suspects confronted a security guard outside the premises before forcing their way in.
Once inside, they intimidated staff and swiftly raided several display counters, removing jewellery before fleeing in the same vehicle they arrived in.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Kepong MP offers RM5,000 reward for recapture of Pakistani fugitive
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A member of parliament has announced a RM5,000 reward for information leading to the recapture of a Pakistani detainee who escaped during a transfer at Sungai Buloh Prison.
Kepong MP Lim Lip Eng made the announcement after sharing his frustration that Malaysians had gone seven days without clear explanation from the authorities on how such escape was possible.
"How does a detainee under full custody simply run off during a transfer?
"How is such a lapse even possible in a high-security environment?
"Enough delay. Enough uncertainty.
"To assist in bringing this fugitive back into custody, I am offering a reward of RM5,000 for credible information leading to his arrest," he said in a statement today.
Lim also referred to a viral video circulating online which appears to show the detainee not being handcuffed at the time of the escape.
"If this is true, it raises serious and troubling questions.
"If it is not, the authorities must immediately clarify.
"Either way, the silence and delay in providing clear answers are unacceptable," he said.
Lim said the incident reflected a serious breakdown in basic security procedures involving individuals under full custody.
He also said that public safety had been compromised and called for full accountability over the incident, including identifying those responsible for the lapse.
Police have opened an internal investigation into
possible breaches of standard operating procedures (SOP)
following the escape of a remand detainee from the Sungai Buloh Prison.
This latest development follows an ongoing large-scale manhunt with authorities believing the suspect remains within the district.
Last week, a Pakistani detainee escaped during a transfer at Sungai Buloh Prison after attending court proceedings at the Shah Alam Court on April 9.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Syria begins loading Iraqi oil shipments for re-export
Article:
SYRIA: Syria began loading its first tanker carrying Iraqi oil on Wednesday at the Baniyas port refinery, according to state media and an AFP correspondent, after Iraq was largely unable to export during the Middle East war.
With maritime traffic through the Strait of Hormuz disrupted, Iraq's exports came to a halt and oil storage tanks began filling up rapidly, forcing Iraqi authorities to largely suspend production.
At the beginning of April, Iraq announced it had started transporting oil by truck through Syria in preparation for re-export by boat.
"The loading of the first oil tanker is underway in Syria today, under the agreement reached with the Iraqi side to transport Iraqi oil to the Baniyas refinery and then to the oil terminal for shipment by sea," Syrian Petroleum Company deputy CEO Ahmed Qubbaji told reporters.
"The quantity that will be loaded onto the tanker is estimated at around 500,000 tons" and the loading operation will take at least three days, he said.
According to Qubbaji, the agreement allows Syria to take "the oil we need for power plants in order to generate electricity, while the surplus is exported."
An AFP correspondent saw dozens of trucks with Iraqi license plates at the entrance to the city of Tartus, heading towards the Baniyas refinery and the storage tanks surrounding it.
The Iraqi oil ministry said in early April that it had begun exporting oil by truck through neighbouring Syria.
A founding member of the OPEC oil cartel, Iraq is hugely dependent on its oil exports, accounting for some 90 percent of its budget revenues.
Before the start of the Middle East war on February 28, most of its output – roughly 3.5 million barrels per day in normal times – had been exported via the Gulf.
Last month, Iraq announced it had resumed part of its exports, up to 250,000 barrels per day, through the Kurdistan region's oil pipeline that runs to Turkey's Ceyhan port.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Anwar: Reopening of Strait of Hormuz vital to ending Middle East crisis
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: The ongoing conflict in the Middle East must end with the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz, backed by clear guarantees to halt hostilities, attacks and sanctions, Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim said.
Tensions in the region have escalated since the United States and Israel launched attacks against Iran on Feb 28, disrupting the vital shipping lane and triggering a global energy crisis.
Anwar said Malaysia has leveraged its close ties with Tehran to convey the concerns of Gulf and Arab leaders to the Iranian leadership, while using its position to promote peace and a negotiated resolution.
However, he noted that legitimate security concerns inevitably arise when a country is attacked without assurances of safety.
"That is why, while supporting freedom of navigation, we also want categorical guarantees from countries — including big powers — to end hostilities, attacks and even sanctions against other countries. We look at it in a more comprehensive manner.
"Ultimately, we are for peace, and the Strait of Hormuz must finally be open to the international community. This will help resolve major issues confronting not only the region, but the world," he said during a joint press conference with Australian Prime Minister Anthony Albanese today.
Albanese, who is on a three-day official visit to Malaysia, earlier held a four-eyed meeting with Anwar, followed by bilateral discussions involving senior ministers and officials.
He said Australia would continue to push for de-escalation through dialogue rather than military action.
"There are consequences of conflict for the loss of innocent lives, but there are also consequences for the entire world due to the extraordinary negative economic impact," he said.
He added that Australia's position on freedom of navigation remains clear under the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea (Unclos), stressing that the principle applies globally as the basis of international trade.
"Freedom of navigation is the basis of international trade, and that is a principle we hold to."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: AI demand drives chipmaker TSMC's net profit to fresh record
Article:
TAIPEI: Taiwanese chip maker TSMC said Thursday its net profit surged to a fresh record in the first quarter, fuelled by the global artificial intelligence race despite the war in the Middle East.
Massive demand for AI hardware means business is booming for TSMC -- the biggest contract maker of microchips used in everything from Apple phones to Nvidia processors.
Chief financial officer Wendell Huang said the company did not expect the war to impact its supply of key chipmaking materials such as helium and hydrogen in the near term.
"We source from multiple suppliers in different regions, and we have prepared safety stock inventory on hand," Huang told an earnings call, adding that energy supplies were also sufficient to continue operations as normal for now.
TSMC said net profit for the first three months of the year jumped 58.3 per cent year-on-year to NT$572.5 billion (US$18 billion), trouncing analyst estimates of NT$540.20 billion.
Governments and tech giants are pouring huge sums into building data centres that can train and run AI tools such as chatbots, image generators and agents that can execute tasks.
"The recent situation in the Middle East... brings further macroeconomic uncertainties, as such we are being prudent in our business planning," TSMC chairman CC Wei said.
"Having said that, AI-related demand continues to be extremely robust," he added.
"We maintain strong confidence for our full-year 2026 revenue to now grow by above 30 per cent in US dollar terms."
Tight helium supply
Last month, Jensen Huang, head of top US chip designer Nvidia, said everyone in the tech world felt they could develop their AI and grow revenue "if they could just get more capacity".
Ahead of Thursday's earnings announcement, Ian Lyall at Proactive Investors said it appeared TSMC is "so deeply embedded in the AI supply chain that macro headwinds are struggling to leave a mark".
"The bleeding-edge manufacturing that only TSMC can reliably deliver at scale is running at capacity," he noted.
A weaker Taiwanese dollar had also boosted the firm's revenues from overseas sales. On Thursday, it said quarterly net revenue rose 35.1 per cent year-on-year to NT$1.13 trillion.
A note from UBS analysts had predicted strong quarterly results for TSMC but warned that consumer demand was weakening as a result of higher prices caused by a global memory chip shortage that is a side-effect of the AI boom.
"Cloud AI demand continues to strengthen, but we think supply constraints will limit meaningful upside for TSMC this year," the UBS team said.
"Middle East tensions add a layer of macro uncertainty, but AI spend should stay insulated, barring a protracted conflict."
The UBS analysts predicted "limited disruption from tight helium supply on TSMC's production".
Helium gas is a key material in the chip supply chain, and Qatar -- one of the countries affected by the war in the Middle East -- is one of its few large-scale producers.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Selangor revises 2026 budget to mitigate Middle East conflict economic impact
Article:
SHAH ALAM: Selangor will revise its 2026 state budget to mitigate the economic impact of the escalating conflict involving the United States, Israel, and Iran, Menteri Besar Datuk Seri Amirudin Shari said today.
The revision, Amirudin said, will focus on addressing the rising cost of living, providing aid to affected people, supporting industries, and accelerating the transition to renewable energy.
Amirudin said the decision followed a Selangor Economic Action Council (MTES) special meeting, which evaluated immediate measures to safeguard the state's economy.
"The closure of the Strait of Hormuz due to the conflict has impacted energy supplies vital for transport, as well as fertiliser and urea production, which are critical for food security.
"It has also affected the supply of chemicals and helium used in hospitals and semiconductor manufacturing," he said in a statement.
To manage the situation, the state government has established the Selangor Energy Crisis Task Force, comprising a panel of distinguished experts.
The panel includes former Bank Negara governor Tan Sri Muhammad Ibrahim as chairman, Malaysian Inclusive Development and Advancement Institute (Minda-UKM) director Professor Tan Sri Dr Noor Azlan Ghazali and former Maybank Islamic Bhd chief executive officer Datuk Mohamed Rafique Merican.
"This task force will propose immediate measures to reduce the impact on the people and draft an action plan for the next year should the war continue," Amirudin said.
The first phase of mitigation efforts for the people in the state is expected to be introduced within the next two weeks.
As an immediate measure to reduce fuel consumption and traffic congestion, the state government has implemented a work-from-home policy for civil servants, effective yesterday.
The policy will apply every Tuesday to Thursday and will be reviewed monthly based on its effectiveness and the prevailing situation.
Amirudin also encouraged the private sector to adopt similar arrangements where feasible, adding that the state is ready to assist in digital technology adaptation.
Taking cue from the federal government austerity measures due to the Middle East conflict, Amirudin said all official overseas travel for state departments has been suspended, except for missions related to attracting foreign investment and tourism to Selangor.
"State departments must coordinate efforts and operate in 'crisis mode' to ensure the local economy and job opportunities remain resilient.
"Every people in Selangor must stand together as we prepare to face the long-term economic challenges brought about by this war," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Police bust counterfeit liquor syndicate in Negri Sembilan, Selangor
Article:
fuadnizam@nst.com.my
KUALA LUMPUR: Police have busted a syndicate involved in the production and packaging of counterfeit liquor in two states, with seizures worth about RM221,000.
Federal police Wildlife Crime Control Bureau/Special Investigation Intelligence (WCB/PSK) commander Datuk Mohd Zaki Ashar said the operation was carried out following two separate raids in Senawang, Negri Sembilan, and Beranang, Selangor, yesterday.
He said the first raid at 3.30pm at premises in the Tuanku Jaafar III Industrial Area in Senawang led to the arrest of five foreign nationals comprising three Nepali men, one Nepali woman and one Myanmar man.
"Seizures in the first raid included 95 boxes of assorted brands of alcoholic beverages, 18 cartons of non-dutiable cigarettes and six boxes of subsidised cooking oil from the Domestic Trade and Cost of Living Ministry in Seremban," he said in a statement.
Following information from the first raid, a second operation was conducted at 5.45pm in Beranang with assistance from the Kajang district police headquarters, he added.
"In the second raid, police detained two foreign nationals from Myanmar and India respectively, who were caught transferring boxes containing suspected counterfeit alcoholic beverages.
"Authorities also seized 69 boxes of assorted liquor brands as well as 22 machines and equipment used for labelling alcoholic products," he said.
Zaki said checks found that all those arrested failed to produce valid documentation and licences for alcohol manufacturing.
They were also found in possession of untaxed cigarettes and subsidised cooking oil, along with other offences related to identification documents.
He said the total value of seizures was estimated at RM221,514.75, with the actual value of items confiscated at RM206,764.75.
All detainees and seized items have been handed over to the Seremban and Kajang district police headquarters for further action.
Zaki said the case is being investigated under Section 135(1)(d) of the Customs Act 1957, Section 6(1)(c) and Section 39B of the Immigration Act 1959/63, as well as the Control of Supplies Act 1961.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] Drug syndicate busted for hiding meth in food tins bound for Sabah
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Police have crippled a drug syndicate that concealed methamphetamine in food tins before attempting to smuggle the drugs into Sabah via courier services, with investigations revealing the network was also targeting lucrative markets in the Philippines.
The bust, carried out under Op Icerya through a series of raids in the Klang Valley on April 15, led to the arrest of a local man and four foreigners, and the seizure of more than RM3 million worth of drugs.
Bukit Aman Narcotic Crime Investigation Department (NCID) director Datuk Hussein Omar Khan said police seized 61kg of methamphetamine worth RM3.05 million.
He said the syndicate, believed to have been active since March, used a deceptive method of repackaging the drugs into food tins to evade detection before shipping them to Sabah as a transit point to Borneo.
"From Sabah, we believe the drugs were meant to be moved further into neighbouring regions, including the southern Philippines, where demand and prices are significantly higher," he said at a press conference at the Kuala Lumpur police headquarters today.
Hussein said methamphetamine in Malaysia is typically valued at about RM50,000 per kg, but can fetch three to four times that amount in the Philippines.
"This price disparity is what drives syndicates to move drugs across borders, as the profits are far more lucrative in external markets," he said, adding that the syndicate would send the drugs to east Malaysian locations using courier services.
He explained that once the drugs were sent to the recipient location, the parcels would be collected by a delivery rider to avoid detection.
"In the first raid in Serdang Perdana, Selangor, police seized 48 tins containing 30.4kg of methamphetamine.
"A follow-up raid at an apartment in Bukit Jalil led to the discovery of another 30.6kg of the same drug," he said.
Hussein said the operation was the result of intelligence sharing between the Malaysian Border Control and Protection Agency (MCBA) in Sabah and the Sabah police headquarters' narcotics division.
He said the syndicate was masterminded by an African national, with each suspect playing specific roles.
"Two suspects acted as runners, one as the main transporter, while another served as the syndicate coordinator," he said.
Investigations revealed that the foreign suspects had entered the country as early as 2014 using student visas, but police believe they also used false identities to carry out their activities.
"All suspects tested negative for drugs and have no prior criminal records," he said.
Police also seized two vehicles, a Honda City and a Hyundai Elantra, under the Dangerous Drugs (Forfeiture of Property) Act, with an estimated value of RM79,000.
Hussein said the total value of drugs seized and assets confiscated in the case amounted to RM3.13 million, adding that the drugs could have affected up to 305,000 users.
All suspects have been remanded for seven days until April 22 to assist investigations under Section 39B of the Dangerous Drugs Act.
Hussein said discussions have been conducted with the Communications Department and courier services association to ensure stricter screenings of parcels.
"We do not want to leave any opportunity or loopholes for these syndicates to take advantage," he said.
Members of the public with information on drug activities can contact the NCID hotline at 012-208 7222.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Drivers' confrontation in PJ resolved, case classified as NFA
Article:
PETALING JAYA: A viral confrontation between two drivers at a traffic light junction along Jalan PJU 8/1 here has been resolved amicably and classified as no further action (NFA).
District police chief, Assistant Commissioner Shamsudin Mamat, said the incident occurred at noon yesterday.
He said both parties had lodged police reports over the matter.
"Investigations found that the incident stemmed from both parties being impatient and refusing to give way while driving.
"Both parties have agreed to resolve the matter amicably and the case has been classified as NFA," he said in a statement.
Earlier, a
video went viral on social media
showing a heated confrontation between two individuals at the traffic light junction, which disrupted traffic flow in the area and drew public attention.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] Ops Langka: Police cripple syndicate, seize RM13.68mil worth of cannabis
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Police have crippled a drug syndicate following a major nationwide crackdown under Ops Langka, arresting 191 individuals and seizing drugs worth RM13.68 million.
Bukit Aman Narcotics Crime Investigation Department (NCID) director Datuk Hussein Omar Khan said the operation, conducted between April 9 and 12, targeted high-risk areas in Kedah, Perlis, Langkawi and Kuala Kedah.
The crackdown followed growing concerns over rising drug abuse and trafficking in the northern states.
Hussein said a total of 231 raids were carried out, resulting in arrests for various offences under the Dangerous Drugs Act 1952 and other related laws.
"The operation was launched following concerns raised by the Home Minister on March 17 last year over the increasing drug problem in Kedah and Perlis.
"Our objective was to dismantle the drug supply chain at all levels, and the results show significant disruption to syndicate activities," he said.
Building on intelligence gathered during Ops Langka, police launched a follow-up operation, dubbed Ops Conops, on April 12 involving two coordinated raids in the Klang Valley, leading to the arrest of seven suspects aged between 28 and 43, including two foreigners.
In the first raid at a condominium parking area in Putrajaya, police arrested four suspects and seized 37 packages of cannabis buds weighing 37.33kg, along with an additional 0.42kg of cannabis.
Further checks at the same condominium unit uncovered a larger cache comprising 131 packages of cannabis buds weighing 133.30kg and 3.26kg of cannabis.
A second raid at a terrace house in Setapak led to the arrest of three more suspects and the seizure of an additional 3.32kg of cannabis and 0.11kg of cannabis buds.
"In total, we seized 170.74kg of cannabis buds and 7.04kg of cannabis, with an estimated street value of RM13.68 million.
"The drugs could have affected up to 356,000 users," Hussein said.
Investigations revealed that the syndicate had been active since December last year, using condominium units in the Klang Valley as storage and repackaging hubs before distributing the drugs to both local and international markets.
"For local distribution, the syndicate used e-hailing and courier services such as Grab and Lalamove, while for international markets, they relied on drug mules who concealed the drugs in luggage.
"We believe the supply originated from the northern region of Peninsular Malaysia," he said.
Police identified the roles of those arrested, including coordinators, runners, packers and individuals responsible for recruiting drug mules and distributing drugs locally.
Urine tests showed several suspects tested positive for multiple drugs, including methamphetamine, morphine, THC and benzodiazepines.
All suspects are being investigated under Section 39B of the Dangerous Drugs Act 1952, which carries the death penalty or life imprisonment and whipping upon conviction.
Five of the suspects have been remanded for seven days until April 19, while two others were released after their remand applications were rejected.
Further checks revealed that several suspects had prior criminal records, including drug-related offences.
Police have also taken action under the Dangerous Drugs (Forfeiture of Property) Act 1988, seizing assets worth RM385,000, including a Honda Civic, jewellery, pawnshop receipts and cash.
"The total value of drugs seized and assets confiscated is estimated at RM14.07 million," Hussein said, adding that investigations are ongoing to track down other members of the syndicate.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Firm used overdraft to pay Bersatu RM1m in 2022, court hears in Muhyiddin abuse trial
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, April 16 — A company which did not have enough funds in its bank account had taken an overdraft to pay out RM1 million to political party Parti Pribumi Bersatu Malaysia in 2022, a banker told the High Court during Tan Sri Muhyiddin Yassin’s power abuse and money laundering trial.
Maybank Kota Kemuning branch’s assistant manager Alfian Othman, 57, confirmed Nepturis Sdn Bhd had taken overdrafts from Maybank in February 2022, including for a RM1 million sum paid to Bersatu.
Alfian explained that an overdraft facility is when a bank customer’s account balance is insufficient, and refers to when the bank has allowed the customer to pay out amounts beyond what is in their account.
In other words, a customer would be borrowing money from the bank to pay out money.
Looking at Nepturis Sdn Bhd’s Maybank February 2022 bank statement, Alfian confirmed that it had a negative account balance during multiple transactions that month, and that one of the biggest sums paid out then was a RM1 million payment to Bersatu’s AmBank account on February 21, 2022 via a Maybank cheque (with cheque number 470023).
Asked by deputy public prosecutor Datuk Wan Shaharuddin Wan Ladin if this RM1 million had entered Bersatu’s account and if “this amount is from the overdraft facility given by the bank”, Alfian replied “yes”.
Earlier, Alfian verified that this RM1 million had indeed entered Bersatu’s AmBank account (account number 8881028867041), based on the latter’s bank statement.
This RM1 million cheque is the exact same cheque that appeared in this trial previously: it is part of 10 cheques totalling RM6 million verified as having entered Bersatu’s AmBank account in 2022, based on AmBank banker Nor Paizah Osman’s court testimony as the third prosecution witness.
Later, when asked by Muhyiddin’s defence lawyer Datuk Amer Hamzah Arshad, Alfian agreed that the RM1 million cheque payment by Nepturis did not refer to Muhyiddin and that the cheque only stated Bersatu as the recipient.
Alfian agreed with Amer that all payments through Nepturis’s bank account are recorded, further agreeing that this meant nothing is hidden and that all transactions would be transparent, and that there is nothing to be hidden about the RM1 million paid to Bersatu.
Earlier today, CIMB Bukit Tunku branch’s assistant manager Herdayanti Md Hasan, as the seventh prosecution witness, verified that the bank branch’s client Azman Yusoff had paid out a total of RM4 million to Bersatu’s CIMB account on two occasions in 2021 and 2022.
CIMB Bukit Tunku branch assistant manager Herdayanti Md Hasan is seen leaving the court after testifying in Tan Sri Muhyiddin Yassin’s corruption case at the Kuala Lumpur Court Complex on April 16, 2026. — Picture by Sayuti Zainudin
Based on both Azman’s CIMB bank statements and Bersatu’s CIMB bank statements, Herdayanti verified that these four cheques from Azman had entered Bersatu’s account:
RM2 million (cheque number 73659) paid on September 24, 2021;
RM1 million (cheque number 73660) paid on September 24, 2021;
RM500,000 (cheque number 73665) paid on November 3, 2022;
RM500,000 (cheque number 73666) paid on November 3, 2022.
These four cheques paid out by Azman to Bersatu have also already appeared in banking records in Muhyiddin’s trial previously: again, these are the exact same cheques that another CIMB banker, Lee Hui Nee, had previously verified as being received by Bersatu.
Herdayanti agreed with Amer that all payments through cheques are transparent and nothing would be hidden as everything would be captured by the banking system.
Among other things, Herdayanti today agreed with Amer that bank records showed that KCJ Engineering Sdn Bhd had previously transferred RM150,000 in three transactions of RM50,000 to Azman, and agreed that Azman had used KCJ Engineering’s address as the address for his personal CIMB bank account’s statement.
On Monday, Maybank’s Shah Alam main branch’s operations officer Chang Hui Chin confirmed that KCJ Engineering Sdn Bhd — where Azman is one of its three authorised signatories — had paid out RM800,000 via four Maybank Islamic cheques to Bersatu.
Chang, who was the sixth prosecution witness, said the four KCJ Engineering cheques of RM200,000 each dated September 1, 2022 were all paid out to Bersatu on October 12, 2022, with the four cheques signed by the company’s two other signatories Zulhelmi Mairin @ Kunting and Mohd Zaid Yusoff.
These four cheques totalling RM800,000 are also part of the 28 cheques totalling RM19.3 million verified by CIMB banker Lee previously.
In this trial, Muhyiddin is facing seven charges, namely four counts of alleged power abuse to obtain RM225.3m bribes for Bersatu (from Nepturis, Azman, Bukhary Equity Sdn Bhd, and Mamfor Sdn Bhd) and three counts of alleged money laundering through money that Bersatu received from Bukhary Equity.
The trial before High Court judge Noor Ruwena Md Nurdin will resume on April 28.
